original_abstract: This volume investigates educational inequalities among children of Turkish immigrants in Austria, France, and Sweden. One of the largest immigrant groups in these countries, Turks nonetheless face discrimination and limited opportunities, and this study shows how those problems play out in education. One of its key findings is that systems that provide more favorable institutional arrangements lead to greater economic mobility in the second generation.
humanized_abstract: The 15 chapters in this volume investigate educational inequalities among children of Turkish immigrants in Austria, France and Sweden. With estimates varying between 1.5 and 2 million, Turks constitute one of the largest immigrant groups in these three Western European countries. They are not only affected by the general exclusion and discrimination they face in society and in the labour market, but their educational careers are also severely affected by these factors, as this volume illustrates in detail. The common research focus and comparative approach of the contributions to this volume provide insight into how the conditions for educational careers differ between these three countries and what consequences these differences have for the second generation’s opportunities to improve their socio-economic situation. A central message of this volume is that there are large differences between the educational systems due to institutional factors that affect the second generation’s economic opportunities in a varying manner.
